trigger: Rapid in-out movement on a recently opened low-activity account

triage.explanation: The alert shows a classic pass-through pattern: a large inbound transfer of 52,000 MYR from Evergreen Logistics Ltd is followed within 2 hours by a nearly equal outbound transfer of 51,500 MYR to MaxCash Money Changer, leaving a minimal balance of 1,100 MYR. The account was recently opened (2026-04-20) and had low prior activity, matching all indicators of PT-01 (Rapid Movement/Pass-through).

matchedTypology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

indicators: Inbound credit followed by outbound debit of a similar amount within a short window (minutes to a few days)
runningBalance spikes on the inbound then drains back to ~0
Little to no funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated purpose of the account
Often a newly opened or recently dormant account
fired: Inbound credit followed by outbound debit of a similar amount within a short window (minutes to a few days)
runningBalance spikes on the inbound then drains back to ~0
Little to no funds retained; no spending consistent with the stated purpose of the account
Often a newly opened or recently dormant account

verifier.note: The transactions show irregular timing (single event), diverse counterparties (logistics and money changer), and no commercial rationale for a personal account, matching the pass-through laundering typology rather than a benign sweep.

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

strDraft.activitySummary: On 2026-06-14, Tan Wei Ming's account received an inbound transfer of 52,000 MYR from Evergreen Logistics Ltd, and within roughly two hours, sent 51,500 MYR to MaxCash Money Changer, leaving a near-zero balance of 1,100 MYR. The running balance spiked on receipt then rapidly drained, indicating a pass-through with no funds retained for any legitimate economic purpose.
groundsForSuspicion: Inbound credit of 52,000 MYR followed by outbound debit of 51,500 MYR within a short window (2 hours 15 minutes).
Running balance returned to near zero (1,100 MYR) immediately after the transactions.
No funds retained; activity inconsistent with stated account purpose and lacking apparent economic rationale.
Account recently opened (2026-04-20) with minimal prior activity, matching pass-through typology.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

typology.name: Pass-through / Rapid Movement
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.10/R.20; BNM AML/CFT Policy Document

The remaining balance fell to MYR 2,700, indicating rapid movement of funds out of the account shortly after receipt.

citedTransactions.counterpartyName: Kopitiam Aroma Sdn Bhd - Operating A/C

trigger: Repeated cash deposits just below the RM25,000 CTR threshold across multiple branches and days

triage.explanation: Account shows structured cash deposits of RM24,200, RM23,800, RM24,100 across three branches over three days, all just under the RM25,000 CTR threshold. Total aggregated RM72,200 then moved out in a single large transfer. This matches structuring/smurfing typology ST-01.

matchedTypology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
matchedTypology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

indicators: Multiple CASH deposits clustered just under the RM25,000 CTR threshold
Repeated similar sub-threshold amounts over a short period
Spread across DAYS, BRANCHES, or multiple PERSONS (smurfing) to defeat same-day aggregation
Total aggregated value is substantial
fired: Multiple CASH deposits clustered just under the RM25,000 CTR threshold
Repeated similar sub-threshold amounts over a short period
Spread across DAYS, BRANCHES, or multiple PERSONS (smurfing) to defeat same-day aggregation
Total aggregated value is substantial

verifier.note: Three cash deposits just below RM25,000 at different branches on consecutive days, followed by a large outbound transfer, with no documented commercial reason, indicating structuring.

typology.name: Structuring / Smurfing
typology.source: FATF Recommendations (2012), R.20; BNM AMLA 2001; CTR threshold RM25,000

strDraft.activitySummary: Between 10 and 12 June 2026, Daniel Choo Kah Wai deposited RM24,200, RM23,800, and RM24,100 in cash at three different branches, each amount just below the RM25,000 reporting threshold. The aggregated sum of RM72,200 was then largely transferred out to Sterling Overseas Trading on the same day as the final deposit.
groundsForSuspicion: Three cash deposits of RM24,200 (10 June, Bukit Bintang), RM23,800 (11 June, Cheras), and RM24,100 (12 June, Petaling Jaya) — all immediately under the RM25,000 CTR threshold.
Deposits were made on consecutive days at different branches, indicating a deliberate attempt to avoid same-day aggregation and detection.
Aggregate cash inflow of RM72,200 was swiftly followed by a single outgoing transfer of RM70,000 to Sterling Overseas Trading, leaving a minimal balance.
The pattern is characteristic of structuring/smurfing to circumvent CTR reporting requirements.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

trigger: Many small inbound payments from numerous parties flagged as fan-in

triage.explanation: Alert triggered for fan-in activity with multiple small card payments from different order numbers within a single day. While no outbound consolidation is shown in the provided evidence, the pattern of numerous unconnected senders depositing funds into a single account matches the initial stage of a fan-in/fan-out typology. Given cost-sensitive detection, escalation is warranted for further analysis.

matchedTypology.name: Fan-in / Fan-out (Consolidation)

Senders have no apparent relationship to the account holder
Amounts and frequency inconsistent with the account holder's profile

strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).

strDraft.activitySummary: Nurul Aina binti Hamzah, a self-declared administrative clerk earning RM3,000 monthly, received an international wire of RM150,000 from a Dubai-based company. Occupation as an administrative clerk does not explain the source, volume, or destination of funds.

Rapid movement of nearly all received funds through layered transfers suggests potential money laundering.
strDraft.recommendedAction: Escalate to the Financial Intelligence and Enforcement Department (FIED).
